title: 48" Wide Closet Units
summary: A prefinished-plywood closet tower with a toekick base, fixed and adjustable shelving, and hardwood cleats for joining multiple towers side by side.
description:
This closet system is built from plywood ripped into consistent 15-3/4"-wide strips with the front edge banded, which is what lets prefinished material look like a genuine built-in rather than a stack of shelves. Drilling the shelf-pin holes is the one step where real care matters more than speed — even a small offset between holes on the two sides will leave every adjustable shelf sitting slightly tilted, and there's no fixing that after the holes are drilled.

Because this system is meant to join multiple towers side by side into a full closet wall, the final installation happens in place rather than on the bench: walls are rarely perfectly square, and the connecting cleats and support boards need to be fit to your actual wall, not to a measurement taken beforehand.

tools.note: Rips the plywood into consistent 15-3/4" strips.
Drills the shelf-pin holes — accuracy here matters more than almost anything else in the build.
For the in-place final installation, since walls are rarely perfectly square.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 15-3/4"-wide strips, edge banded on the front, matching species
materials.note: Roughly two sheets per organizer if the layout is planned well.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, matching species, for the back
1x4 board, 8 ft, pine
materials.note: The underneath support — a cheaper species is fine since it's hidden.

materials.name: 1x2 board, 8 ft, matching wood species
materials.note: Installed under the shelf fronts when joining towers together.

cutList.note: Notch the bottom for the toekick.

steps.title: Rip the strips and cut the panels
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 15-3/4"-wide strips on the table saw — every carcass part except the toekick and the back comes off these strips, so a strip that wanders in width puts the whole tower out. From them cut two sides at 84", two top and bottom panels at 46-1/2", two dividers at 79", two large fixed shelves at 23", four small fixed shelves at 11", and twelve adjustable shelves at 10-3/4". Cut the 48" toekick from the 1x4 in your matching species and the 46-1/2" underneath support from the pine 1x4, and cut the 1/4" back to 48" x 84" — leave its factory corner intact, because that square corner is what you will register the carcass against later. Count your material before you start: those parts need seven 96" strips and a 4x8 sheet only yields three, so two sheets will leave you short and a third is needed.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 15-3/4"-wide strips, edge banded on the front, matching species
1/4" plywood, matching species, for the back

steps.title: Notch the sides and drill shelf-pin holes
steps.body: Notch the bottom front corner of both 84" side panels 3-1/2" high to clear the toekick — that depth is what makes the numbers close, since the 79" dividers are the 84" side less the 3-1/2" notch and less 3/4" of bottom panel and 3/4" of top panel. Then lay out the shelf-pin holes. Clamp both sides together face to face with their bottom ends flush and drill the two panels in one operation, or index every hole off the same bottom edge with a stop block, so a hole on the left side and its partner on the right are at identical heights. Use a depth stop on the bit — a pin hole drilled through a 3/4" panel shows on the outside of the tower and cannot be undone. This is the step where care beats speed: even a small offset side to side leaves every adjustable shelf sitting tilted, and there is no fixing it afterward.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 15-3/4"-wide strips, edge banded on the front, matching species

steps.title: Attach the top and bottom to the sides
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of the 46-1/2" top and bottom panels and fasten them between the two sides with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, so the 84" sides run the full outside height and the tower finishes 48" wide — 46-1/2" plus 3/4" of side at each end, which is exactly the 48" width of the back panel. Set the bottom panel on top of the 3-1/2" toekick notch, not at the very bottom of the sides. Face the pocket holes where they will be hidden by the shelves and the back, and clamp each joint tight before driving, since plywood end grain will let a screw pull the panel out of flush.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 15-3/4"-wide strips, edge banded on the front, matching species

steps.title: Attach the support and toekick
steps.body: Attach the underneath support and toekick to the base. Drill pocket holes on the insides, facing upward, to attach to the bottom shelf — with prefinished material every screw hole needs to be hidden this way. If you're painting, you can simply nail or screw from the top instead.

steps.title: Attach the dividers
steps.body: Stand the two 79" dividers between the top and bottom panels and fasten them with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws top and bottom. Their positions are fixed by the shelves: the 46-1/2" interior less two 3/4" dividers leaves 45", which splits as an 11" bay, a 23" bay and an 11" bay — matching the 11" small fixed shelves in the outer bays and the 23" large fixed shelves in the middle. Measure each bay at both the top and the bottom of the divider and clamp a shelf offcut in as a spacer while you drive, or the dividers will bow and the adjustable shelves will bind partway up. Drill the shelf-pin holes in the divider faces now if you want adjustability in the outer bays — it is far easier before the back goes on.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 15-3/4"-wide strips, edge banded on the front, matching species

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Lay the tower face down, put the 48" x 84" back on, and align one factory edge and corner of the back to one side and to the top of the carcass. Nail through the back into every side, divider and fixed shelf with 3/4" finish nails, working outward from that registered corner and pulling the carcass into line with the back's edges as you go. The back is what squares the whole tower — a 1/4" panel cut square is a better reference than any measurement you can take across a 7 ft case.
materials: 1/4" plywood, matching species, for the back

steps.title: Cut and fit the shelves
steps.body: Install the four 11" small fixed shelves in the outer bays and the two 23" large fixed shelves in the middle bay, fastening them through the sides and dividers. Then set the twelve 10-3/4" adjustable shelves on their pins: they are the 11" bay less 1/4", so they belong in the outer bays only and that quarter inch is the clearance that lets you angle one into place without scraping the finish off both walls. Buy more pins before this step — twelve adjustable shelves take four pins each, forty-eight in total, and the plan lists twenty-four. Set a shelf on its pins and check it for rock before loading the tower; a shelf that rocks means a pin hole is off and it is better found now.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 15-3/4"-wide strips, edge banded on the front, matching species

steps.title: Join towers and install
steps.body:
To join multiple towers side by side, attach hardwood cleats to the wall underneath each shelf — use a species matching your prefinished plywood if it's staying natural, or pine, whitewood, MDF, or poplar if you're painting. Add 1x2 support pieces under the shelf fronts as well. Set the shelves on top of the cleats and screw down.

Do this fitting in place rather than on the bench, since walls are rarely perfectly square and the cleats need to match your actual wall.

images.alt: 48-inch wide prefinished plywood closet unit with adjustable shelving
